dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a 911 call for breathing problems at 34th and Jackson.
Hi, I'm Mark, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having a lot of trouble catching my breath. It just got really bad an hour ago.
Can you tell me your name?
And stand a second please-can you follow my finger?
My name is Sarah... yes, I can follow it.
Good. You know where you are, yes? What city are we in now?
She is alert and oriented x4. Her GCS is 15-eye, verbal, and motor responses are normal.
Can you squeeze my fingers with both hands?
She acknowledges and responds quickly. She seems well-oriented, but anxious.
Keep breathing the way you are. Can we take a quick look at your throat to see how you're doing?
Yes... I just want to get air.
She's breathing fast and using her neck muscles. Upper breath sounds are diminished, lower ones are absent.
Respirations are 20. We're starting continuous SpO2 monitoring.
Your pulse is pretty fast, but it's strong and regular on both sides. They feel bounding.
Pulse of 113-higher than normal. Rapid but equal bilaterally.
Now we'll take your blood pressure.
SpO2 is 95. That's a good start, we're on track.
Breathing is rapid and labored. Accessory muscles are heavily used.
No. I just can't get enough air. It's tight in my chest, like a belt around my lungs.
We need to get your full story. Can you answer a few more questions?
What symptoms do you have today besides shortness of breath?
I need to sit up to even get air. I feel worse with each minute.
Have you had shortness breath in the past?
I have a history of chronic breathing problems, but this is much worse.
How long has this been worse today? When did you first notice it?
I woke up with some issues, and it became serious around 1:30.
Who's with you? Anyone brought you here?
I'm her daughter. I called 911 because she was gasping for air.
What medications are you used to taking?
I take some blood pressure pills and medicine for my kidney issues. I didn't take them today.
She has a history of COPD and heart failure, along with kidney disease.
Do you have any allergies?
No known allergies.
Understand. What happened before your breathing got bad?
She came home from rehab last night after being in the hospital for similar breathing issues.
That's good, Sarah-breathe with us. What position helps?
I prefer to be sitting up and leaning forward. It makes breathing a little easier.
We're going to give you some medicine into your breathing system to help get more air.
We're giving albuterol and ipratropium now via nebulizer.
Performing a 4-lead EKG. Don't worry, keep breathing slowly.
We'll take a quick look in your eyes to make sure you understand where we are.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Can you move your legs normally? How is the sensation and strength?
My legs are okay, but they feel a bit swollen. Strength and coordination are equal.
I see some swelling in both legs. Is it itchy or breaking out?
No, just a little tight.
We need to check your stomach. Can you raise your shirt a little?
There's some mild distention in the belly. It feels firm but no pain reported.
There is mild abdominal rigidity but it's not painful.
Blood pressure is 255 over 153-elevated and labile.
We're going to check your blood sugar.
Moving to a head elevated position to help ease breathing.
You're going to receive a small shot to help manage your breathing.
We are going to connect a CPAP to help your breathing more comfortably.
Pulse is now 116, respirations are 25, and SpO2 is 88.
The CPAP pressure has helped with posture and breathing.
We're rechecking your breathing to see if any changes are necessary.
No pain is reported; the condition is still focused on airway stabilization.
BP is now 103 over 59-lower than before.
Your eyes are normal in size and shape-PERRL. No signs of jugular distension.
Normocephalic head, no deformity, and the neck shows no signs of stiffness or tenderness.
I see a dialysis catheter in your left upper chest. It is in place, and the patient feels no pain from it.
Your skin is dry and has a normal color. Mild edema in legs noted.
You have an AV fistula in your right arm and normal coordination and strength in both arms. Peripheral pulses are strong; pos PMS noted.
Taking a 12-lead EKG now for more detailed heart tracing. No pain reported.
EKG remains sinus tachycardia, with minimal ST depression. Respirations show signs of hypocapnia.
She has no pain, and breathing is still labored but stable on CPAP.
The protocol for acute COPD exacerbation with pulmonary edema fits the presentation and interventions we're using.
Are you okay with being transported to [COMPANY_NAME]?
Yes, they know what to do.
I got you secured on the stretcher with your legs and head elevated.
We'll keep monitoring your breathing and new medications while we move to the vehicle. Let's go.
Pulse is 113 now, fairly stable with some improvement in pressure. O2 has improved to 95.
We'll make another round of checks while heading to the hospital.
CPAP continues to support your breathing well.
We gave another dose of albuterol via nebulizer for airway support.
A dose of dexamethasone has been administered intramuscularly for further airway support.
A dose of epinephrine intramuscular has been given to address ongoing respiratory issues.
BP is back up to 258 over 154, with tachypnea persisting at 28 per minute.
SpO2 and pulse show progress, with no pain reported.
We'll follow the acute respiratory distress protocol while on the way.
She is secure on the stretcher in a Fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts.
Vehicle is on the way, and we will begin another round of assessments.
Pulse is at 111, SpO2 at 91-good improvement from earlier readings.
CPAP is keeping oxygen saturation steady and breath sounds improving, per EKG and SpO2.
We're stable and heading to the ER. Hospital was alerted with full vitals and meds.
Additional restraints have been applied for safe head and back support.
CPAP and nebulizer are continuing to help with breathing.
We're on schedule for the emergency department. They've been given a full pre-arrival message.
Patient is secured and showing no signs of distress during the ride.
Condition is steady at the moment. No pain, and no further medication is needed for now.
GCS is 15 as confirmed by your oriented and coherent speech.
No signs of confusion-alert and oriented throughout.
Both pupils are full, reactive to light, and equal in size (PERRL).
Interfacility transport is stable with full vitals already logged.
CPAP and nebuliser continue to work well for respiratory support.
Hospital has been alerted in full with known issues and plans for arrival.
Arrival is imminent. Med staff are ready with your notes.
Final check before handover: breathing is steady, vitals up to date.
All is ready for transfer at the hospital emergency entry point.
Records and treatment plan are ready for the hospital team.
You're in great hands now. We did everything possible to stabilize you.
Patient remains pain-free, breathing is better, with SpO2 at 91.
No signs or reports of pain detected during the entire assessment.
Your skin is warm and dry, with no signs of cyanosis.
You are in a tripod position, which helps you breathe more comfortably.
We found positive peripheral pulses and normal capillary refill time.
